title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume each input has exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.
hints: Think about what information you need to find the complement.
For each number x, you need to find target - x.
A hash map lets you look up values in O(1).
approach: Use a hash map to store each number and its index as we iterate. For each element, check if its complement (target - num) already exists in the map.

title: Valid Parentheses
description: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. An input string is valid if open brackets are closed by the same type of brackets in the correct order.
hints: Use a stack to track opening brackets.
When you see a closing bracket, check if it matches the top of the stack.
At the end, the stack should be empty.
approach: Push opening brackets onto a stack. When a closing bracket is encountered, pop from the stack and verify it matches. If stack is empty at the end, the string is valid.

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
description: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists list1 and list2. Merge the two lists into one sorted list and return the head of the merged linked list.
hints: Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases.
Compare the current nodes of both lists and attach the smaller one.
After one list is exhausted, attach the remainder of the other.
approach: Use a dummy head node. Compare the two list heads, attach the smaller node to the result, and advance that list's pointer. Append remaining nodes once one list is exhausted.

title: Binary Search
description: Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search target in nums. If target exists, return its index. Otherwise, return -1. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.
hints: Maintain a left and right pointer.
Calculate mid = (left + right) // 2.
If nums[mid] == target, return mid. If smaller, move left. If larger, move right.
approach: Classic binary search. Keep halving the search space by comparing the middle element with the target.

title: Maximum Subarray
description: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum. A subarray is a contiguous non-empty sequence of elements within an array.
hints: Kadane's algorithm: track current sum and global max.
If current sum goes negative, reset it to 0 (start fresh).
At each step: currentSum = max(num, currentSum + num)
approach: Kadane's Algorithm: iterate through the array keeping track of the current running sum. If adding the current element makes things worse than starting fresh, reset. Track the global maximum throughout.

title: Reverse Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the reversed list.
hints: You need three pointers: prev, curr, next.
At each step, save next, flip the arrow, then advance all three.
When curr is None, prev is the new head.
approach: Iterative approach using three pointers. At each step, reverse the next pointer of the current node, then advance all pointers forward.

solutions.python:
def reverseList(head):
    prev, curr = None, head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    return prev

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values (i.e., from left to right, level by level).
hints: Use a queue (BFS) — process nodes level by level.
At the start of each level, the queue size = number of nodes in that level.
Process exactly queue.size() nodes, then move to the next level.
approach: BFS using a queue. At each iteration, process all nodes at the current level (queue size at start of iteration), add their children to the queue, and record the level's values.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given an m x n 2D binary grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.
hints: Iterate through each cell. When you find a '1', it's a new island.
DFS/BFS from that cell to mark all connected '1's as visited.
Increment island count each time you start a new DFS.
approach: Iterate through the grid. When a '1' is found, increment the count and DFS to mark all connected land cells as '0' (visited). This avoids double counting.

title: Number of 1 Bits
description: Given a positive integer n, write a function that returns the number of set bits in its binary representation (also known as the Hamming weight).
hints: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit each time.
Count how many times you can do this before n becomes 0.
Built-ins: bin(n).count('1') in Python, bits.OnesCount32 in Go, Integer.bitCount in Java.
approach: Use n & (n-1) to clear the lowest set bit each iteration. Count iterations until n reaches 0. This is O(k) where k is the number of set bits — faster than checking all 32 bits.

solutions.python:
def hammingWeight(n: int) -> int:
    count = 0
    while n:
        n &= n - 1  # clears lowest set bit
        count += 1
    return count

# One-liner
# return bin(n).count('1')

title: Counting Bits
description: Given an integer n, return an array ans of length n + 1 such that for each i (0 <= i <= n), ans[i] is the number of 1's in the binary representation of i.
hints: i >> 1 is i with the last bit removed — you already computed its count.
The last bit (i & 1) is either 0 or 1, just add it.
approach: DP: for each i, shift right by 1 (drop last bit) — already computed — then add the last bit (i & 1). This builds the answer in O(n) using prior results.

title: Reverse Bits
description: Reverse bits of a given 32-bit unsigned integer.
hints: Extract the last bit of n with (n & 1), put it into result.
Shift result left and n right by 1 each iteration.
Repeat 32 times — one per bit.
approach: Loop 32 times. Each iteration: shift result left by 1, OR in the lowest bit of n (n & 1), then shift n right by 1. After 32 iterations result holds the reversed bits.

title: Missing Number
description: Given an array nums containing n distinct numbers in the range [0, n], return the only number in the range that is missing from the array.
hints: Math: expected sum = n*(n+1)/2. Answer = expected - actual sum.
XOR: XOR all indices 0..n and all values. Paired values cancel out, leaving the missing one.
Math approach is simpler and O(1) space.
approach: Gauss formula: expected sum of [0..n] is n*(n+1)/2. Subtract the actual array sum to get the missing number. XOR alternative: XOR all indices and all values — everything cancels except the missing index.

title: Sum of Two Integers
description: Given two integers a and b, return the sum of the two integers without using the operators + and -.
hints: XOR (a ^ b) gives the sum bits without carrying.
AND shifted left ((a & b) << 1) gives the carry bits.
Repeat until there is no carry (b == 0).
approach: XOR gives sum without carry; AND<<1 gives the carry. Add carry into the partial sum by repeating until carry is 0. In Python, mask to 32 bits to handle Python's arbitrary-precision integers.

title: Rotate Image
description: You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image. Rotate the image by 90 degrees clockwise in-place.
hints: 90° clockwise = transpose then reverse each row.
Transpose: swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i] for j > i.
Then reverse each row in-place.
approach: Two-step in-place: (1) Transpose the matrix by swapping matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]. (2) Reverse each row. This is equivalent to a 90° clockwise rotation.

title: Spiral Matrix
description: Given an m x n matrix, return all elements of the matrix in spiral order.
hints: Use four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right.
Traverse: right along top, down along right, left along bottom, up along left. Then shrink boundaries.
Check top <= bottom and left <= right before each pass to avoid duplicates.
approach: Maintain four boundary pointers (top, bottom, left, right). Traverse each layer: go right → down → left → up, then shrink all four boundaries inward. Guard the left/up passes to avoid re-visiting cells in non-square matrices.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
description: Given an m x n integer matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0's. You must do it in place.
hints: First pass: record which rows and columns contain a zero.
Second pass: zero out those rows and columns.
O(1) space trick: use the first row and first column as your markers.
approach: Two-pass approach: first collect all (row, col) positions of zeros into sets. Second pass: if row or col is in the sets, set to 0. O(1) space upgrade: use matrix[0][c] and matrix[r][0] as flags, being careful to handle the first row/column separately.

title: Insert Interval
description: Given an array of non-overlapping intervals sorted by start time and a newInterval, insert the interval and merge if necessary. Return the resulting array.
hints: Add all intervals ending before newInterval starts
Merge all overlapping intervals
Add remaining intervals after newInterval ends
approach: Iterate through. Add intervals that end before new one starts. Merge all overlapping by expanding newInterval. Add the rest.

title: Merge Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals.
hints: Sort intervals by start time first
Compare each interval's start with the last merged interval's end
Merge if overlapping, else add new interval
approach: Sort by start. Iterate and merge if current start <= last merged end, otherwise append.

title: Non-overlapping Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals, return the minimum number of intervals you need to remove to make the rest of the intervals non-overlapping.

examples.explanation: Remove [1,3] and the rest are non-overlapping

hints: Sort by end time, not start time
Greedy: always keep the interval that ends earliest
Count intervals that can't be kept (they overlap with previous)
approach: Greedy: sort by end time. Keep an interval if its start >= prevEnd. Count removals otherwise.

title: Meeting Rooms
description: Given an array of meeting time intervals where intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i], determine if a person could attend all meetings.
hints: Sort intervals by start time
Check if any meeting starts before the previous one ends
One overlap means false
approach: Sort by start. If any interval starts before the previous ends, return false.

title: Meeting Rooms II
description: Given an array of meeting time intervals, return the minimum number of conference rooms required.
hints: Use a min-heap to track end times of ongoing meetings
Sort by start time
If earliest ending meeting ends before next starts, reuse that room
Heap size at end = number of rooms needed
approach: Sort by start. Use min-heap of end times. If heap top <= current start, pop (reuse room). Push current end. Heap size = rooms needed.

title: Shortest Path with Alternating Colors
description:
You are given an integer n, the number of nodes in a directed graph where the nodes are labeled from 0 to n - 1. Each edge is red or blue in this graph, and there could be self-edges and parallel edges.

You are given two arrays redEdges and blueEdges where:
- redEdges[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that there is a directed red edge from node ai to node bi.
- blueEdges[i] = [ui, vi] indicates that there is a directed blue edge from node ui to node vi.

Return an array answer of length n, where answer[x] is the length of the shortest path from node 0 to node x such that the edge colors alternate along the path, or -1 if no such path exists.
hints: Use BFS where the state is (node, last_edge_color) — you must alternate colors at each step.
Start node 0 with both RED and BLUE as possible first-edge colors simultaneously.
Track visited (node, color) pairs to avoid cycles — the same node can be visited twice, once per color.
approach: BFS with state (node, last_color). Initialize the queue with (0, RED, 0) and (0, BLUE, 0). At each step expand neighbors reachable by the opposite color. Mark (node, color) visited. The first time a node is reached its distance is optimal.

title: Special Binary String
description:
Special binary strings are binary strings with the following two properties:
- The number of 0's equals the number of 1's.
- Every prefix of the binary string has at least as many 1's as 0's.

You are given a special binary string s. A move consists of choosing two consecutive special strings and swapping them. Return the lexicographically largest string that can be obtained after any number of moves.

examples.explanation: The special substrings are "10", "1100", and the whole string. Sorting inner substrings of the outer wrapper descending gives "1100" then "10", producing "11100100".

hints: A special binary string always starts with 1 and ends with 0 — treat 1 as '(' and 0 as ')': it's a valid parenthesization.
Find all top-level special substrings by tracking balance (1 → +1, 0 → -1); each time balance hits 0 you've found one.
Recursively sort inner content of each special substring, then sort the resulting strings in reverse lexicographic order and concatenate.
approach: Map 1→'(' and 0→')'. Scan for top-level special substrings (balance returns to 0). For each, strip the outer '1'…'0', recursively sort the interior, then wrap back. Sort the collected substrings in descending order and join — greedy: putting the lexicographically largest substring first always wins.

title: Neighboring Bitwise XOR
description:
A 0-indexed array derived with length n is derived by computing the bitwise XOR of adjacent values in a binary array original of length n.

Specifically, for each index i in the range [0, n-1]:
- If i = n-1, then derived[i] = original[i] XOR original[0].
- Otherwise, derived[i] = original[i] XOR original[i+1].

Given the array derived, return true if there exists a valid binary array original that could have produced derived, false otherwise.
hints: XOR-expand derived: each original[i] appears exactly twice across all derived[i] = original[i] ^ original[i+1].
Therefore XOR of all derived elements = 0 if and only if a valid original exists.
Equivalent: sum(derived) % 2 == 0, since all values are 0 or 1.
approach: XOR all elements of derived. Because each original[i] appears in exactly two terms (derived[i-1] and derived[i]), every element cancels out, making the total XOR equal 0 iff a valid original exists. O(n) single pass.

solutions.python:
def doesValidArrayExist(derived: list[int]) -> bool:
    xor = 0
    for d in derived:
        xor ^= d
    return xor == 0

# One-liner
# from functools import reduce; return reduce(lambda a, b: a ^ b, derived) == 0

title: Maximum Difference by Remapping a Digit
description:
You are given an integer num. Bob will remap one of the 10 possible digits (0–9) to another digit. When Bob remaps digit d1 to d2, all occurrences of d1 in num become d2. The resulting value may have leading zeros, which are ignored when converting back to an integer.

Return the difference between the maximum and minimum values Bob can make by remapping exactly one digit in num.
hints: To maximize: find the first digit that is not '9' and replace all occurrences with '9'.
To minimize: replace all occurrences of the leading digit with '0' (leading zeros are dropped).
Use string replace for all occurrences, then parse back to integer.
approach: Greedy string manipulation. Max value: scan left-to-right for the first digit ≠ '9', replace all of them with '9'. Min value: replace every occurrence of the leading digit with '0'. Subtract min from max.
timeComplexity: O(d) where d = number of digits

title: Count the Number of Powerful Integers
description:
You are given three integers start, finish, and limit. You are also given a 0-indexed string s representing a positive integer.

A positive integer x is called powerful if:
- x ends with s (s is a suffix of x), and
- every digit of x is at most limit.

Return the total number of powerful integers in the range [start, finish].

examples.explanation: Powerful numbers: 124, 1124, 2124, 3124, 4124. All end with '124' and have digits ≤ 4.
3000 is outside [1000,2000] and 13000 is also out of range.

hints: Use count(finish) - count(start-1) where count(n) = powerful integers in [1, n].
For count(n): fix the suffix to s; build the prefix digit-by-digit using a tight-bound constraint.
Also sum over all shorter valid lengths: for each length L < len(str(n)), count all valid L-digit powerful numbers directly.
approach: Digit DP with tight constraint. count(n) accumulates: (1) all powerful numbers with fewer digits than n by direct formula (first prefix digit 1..limit, rest 0..limit); (2) for the exact length of n, walk digit-by-digit keeping a tight flag — free branches contribute (limit+1)^remaining each, the tight path checks if the suffix fits.
timeComplexity: O(d²) where d = digits of finish

title: Minimum Cost to Make Array Equal
description:
You are given two 0-indexed arrays nums and cost, each of length n. You can do the following operation any number of times:
- Increase or decrease any element of nums by 1. The cost of one operation on element i is cost[i].

Return the minimum total cost to make all elements of nums equal.
hints: The total cost function f(v) = Σ cost[i] × |nums[i] - v| is convex — it decreases then increases.
Binary search on v: if f(mid) > f(mid+1), the minimum is to the right; otherwise to the left or at mid.
Alternatively, find the weighted median: sort by value, accumulate cost until cumulative cost ≥ total/2.
approach: The cost function is convex and piecewise linear. Binary search on the target value in [min(nums), max(nums)]: compare f(mid) vs f(mid+1) to decide which half contains the minimum. Each evaluation is O(n), giving O(n log(maxVal)) overall.

title: Smallest Missing Multiple of K
description:
Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the smallest positive multiple of k that is missing from nums.

A multiple of k is any positive integer divisible by k.

examples.explanation: Multiples of 2: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12... The array contains 2, 4, 6, 8 but not 10.
The smallest multiple of 3 is 3, which is not in the array.

hints: Put all elements of nums into a hash set for O(1) lookup.
Iterate multiples of k starting from k and return the first one not in the set.
The answer is at most (n+1)*k where n = len(nums), so the loop always terminates.
approach: Build a hash set from nums. Then check multiples of k in order (k, 2k, 3k, …) and return the first that is absent. The loop runs at most n+1 times since at most n multiples can be present.

title: Linked List Cycle
description: Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. There is a cycle if some node can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer. Return true if there is a cycle, otherwise false.

examples.explanation: Tail connects back to node at index 1.
Tail connects back to node at index 0.

hints: Use two pointers: slow moves one step, fast moves two steps.
If they ever meet, there is a cycle.
If fast reaches null, there is no cycle.
approach: Floyd's cycle detection (tortoise and hare). Move slow one step and fast two steps each iteration. If the list has a cycle, fast will eventually lap slow and they meet. If fast reaches null the list is acyclic.

title: Reorder List
description: You are given the head of a singly linked list. The list can be represented as L0 → L1 → … → Ln-1 → Ln. Reorder it to: L0 → Ln → L1 → Ln-1 → L2 → Ln-2 → … You may not modify the values in the list's nodes. Only nodes themselves may be changed.
hints: Find the middle of the list using slow/fast pointers.
Reverse the second half in-place.
Merge the two halves by alternating nodes.
approach: Three steps: (1) find the middle with slow/fast pointers; (2) reverse the second half; (3) interleave nodes from the first and reversed second halves. All O(n) time and O(1) space.

solutions.python:
def reorderList(head: ListNode) -> None:
    # Step 1: find middle
    slow, fast = head, head
    while fast.next and fast.next.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next

    # Step 2: reverse second half
    prev, curr = None, slow.next
    slow.next = None
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt

    # Step 3: merge
    first, second = head, prev
    while second:
        tmp1, tmp2 = first.next, second.next
        first.next = second
        second.next = tmp1
        first = tmp1
        second = tmp2

title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
description: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.
hints: Use a dummy head node to handle edge cases (removing the first node).
Advance a fast pointer n+1 steps ahead of slow.
When fast reaches null, slow.next is the node to remove.
approach: One-pass two-pointer. Use a dummy node before head. Advance fast n+1 steps from dummy. Then move both slow and fast together until fast is null. slow.next is the target — set slow.next = slow.next.next.

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
description: You are given an array of k linked-lists lists, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it.
hints: A min-heap of size k lets you always extract the smallest current node in O(log k).
Alternatively, use divide and conquer: merge pairs of lists repeatedly until one remains.
Naive repeated merge of all lists gives O(kN) — aim for O(N log k).
approach: Min-heap approach: push the head of each non-null list into a min-heap keyed by node value. Repeatedly pop the minimum node, append it to the result, and push its next node (if any). Total: O(N log k) where N is total nodes.

title: Invert Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree, and return its root.
hints: Swap the left and right children of each node.
Recurse into both subtrees — order doesn't matter since swaps are independent.
Works iteratively with a queue (BFS) or stack (DFS) as well.
approach: Recursive DFS: for each node, swap its left and right children, then recurse into both. Base case is null. The recursion visits every node exactly once.
spaceComplexity: O(h) recursion stack, O(n) worst case for skewed tree

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth. The maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
hints: The depth of a tree is 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)).
Base case: null node has depth 0.
Can also solve iteratively with BFS, counting levels.
approach: Recursive DFS: return 0 for null, otherwise return 1 + max(maxDepth(left), maxDepth(right)). Each node is visited once.

title: Same Tree
description: Given the roots of two binary trees p and q, write a function to check if they are the same or not. Two binary trees are considered the same if they are structurally identical, and the nodes have the same value.
hints: If both nodes are null, return true.
If exactly one is null, or their values differ, return false.
Recursively check left and right subtrees.
approach: Recursive DFS: two trees are the same iff their roots have equal values and their left and right subtrees are recursively the same. Three base cases: both null → true, one null → false, values differ → false.

title: Subtree of Another Tree
description: Given the roots of two binary trees root and subRoot, return true if there is a subtree of root with the same structure and node values as subRoot and false otherwise. A subtree of a binary tree is a tree that consists of a node in tree and all of this node's descendants.
hints: For each node in root, check if the subtree rooted there is identical to subRoot.
Reuse isSameTree as a helper.
The check is: isSameTree(root, subRoot) || isSubtree(root.left, subRoot) || isSubtree(root.right, subRoot).
approach: DFS with isSameTree helper. At each node of root, check if the tree rooted there equals subRoot. If yes, return true. Otherwise recurse into left and right children. Time is O(m*n) where m and n are the sizes of the two trees.
